findings.title: Rust omits descendant-attribution rescue for bottom slice filters
findings.claim: Requirement A1-10 and the parity matrix claim that both `slice:<name>` polarities honor descendant attribution. Python explicitly performs that rescue, while Rust tests only direct frame ownership with descendant attributes disabled.
findings.reproduction: I used one 70-ns stack `CacheClient#get -> TelemetryWrapper#call -> ComponentRenderer#render -> RequestHandler#render_response`, slices `components` and `instrumentation`, and descendant rule `<name:TelemetryWrapper#call,to:instrumentation`. With `--filter slice:instrumentation`, Python exited 0 with `{"matching_pct":100.0,"matching_time_ns":70,"total_time_ns":70}`; Rust exited 0 with `{"matching_pct":0.0,"matching_time_ns":0,"total_time_ns":70}`. With `--filter '!slice:instrumentation'`, Python emitted 0 matching ns while Rust emitted 70 matching ns.

findings.title: Accepted Python-dialect regexes have different matching semantics in Rust
findings.claim: The two engines successfully compile several common patterns but return different matches because Python `re` and `fancy-regex` do not share Python's anchor, case-folding, and Unicode character-class semantics.
findings.reproduction: I ran identical one-sample target projections with a `Matched` category. Both CLIs exited 0, but these accepted cases differed: filename `/app/x\n`, pattern `regex:x$` produced Python `["Matched"]` versus Rust `["Other"]`; filename `/İ`, pattern `regex:(?i)i` produced Matched versus Other; filename `/\u0301`, pattern `regex:\w` produced Other versus Matched; filename `/\u001f`, pattern `regex:\s` produced Matched versus Other. Because every pattern compiled in both engines, the documented common-subset restriction does not explain the divergence.

findings.title: Recursive and deeply nested inputs escape Python's JSON error boundary
findings.claim: Python only contracts selected exception types and recursively converts YAML metadata without cycle protection. Valid YAML aliases and deeply nested JSON therefore produce exit-1 tracebacks instead of the required exit-2 JSON envelope.
findings.reproduction: For `slices --facts ... --slices ...`, I supplied metadata `metadata: &a\n  x: *a`. Python exited 1 with a traceback ending `RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded`; Rust exited 2 with `{"error": "recursion limit exceeded at line 4 column 13", "ok": false}`. I also supplied facts JSON containing 1,100 nested arrays to `targets --facts ... --target X`: Python exited 1 ending `RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded while decoding a JSON array from a unicode string`, while Rust exited 2 with `{"error": "recursion limit exceeded at line 1 column 128", "ok": false}`.

findings.title: Rust scope top-N selection alphabetizes equal-cost functions
findings.claim: A remaining `BTreeMap` changes the first-seen tie behavior required by A1-18 before `--top` truncation, so Rust and Python select different functions from identical facts.
findings.reproduction: I projected two equal 10-ns samples encountered as `ZOwner` then `AOwner`, sharing the same owner file and scope, and ran `scopes --top 1`. Python exited 0 with `functions: {"ZOwner":{"count":1,"cpu_time":10}}`; Rust exited 0 with `functions: {"AOwner":{"count":1,"cpu_time":10}}`. Sorting only by cost is stable in both implementations, but Rust's source map has already alphabetized the entries.

findings.title: Integral `by_slice` floats beyond int64 are silently clamped only in Rust
findings.claim: Python preserves an integral configuration float's integer spelling and then rejects it outside int64, while Rust clamps it to an int64 endpoint and proceeds with an ordinary report.
findings.reproduction: Using a one-slice, 10-ns fixture and YAML `by_slice: 1.0e20`, Python exited 2 with `{"error": "--by-slice values must be integers.", "ok": false}`. Rust exited 0 and emitted a normal report containing `summary.total_time_ns: 10` and an `App` slice with `time_ns: 10`. The Rust conversion explicitly clamps the float before casting.

findings.title: Tagged YAML metadata is non-parity and nondeterministic
findings.claim: Both loaders accept explicit YAML tags, but Python stringifies unsupported constructed objects while Rust serializes them structurally. Python sets are also rendered in hash-randomized order, violating deterministic artifact and byte-parity claims.
findings.reproduction: With slice metadata `x: !!binary SGVsbG8=`, Python exited 0 with `metadata.x = "b'Hello'"`; Rust exited 0 with `metadata.x = "SGVsbG8="`. With `x: !!set {a: null, b: null}`, Python emitted `metadata.x = "{'b', 'a'}"`, while Rust emitted `metadata.x = {"a":null,"b":null}`. Running a four-member `!!set` fixture in 20 fresh Python processes produced 12 distinct strings, including `{'gamma', 'beta', 'alpha', 'delta'}` and `{'beta', 'alpha', 'gamma', 'delta'}`.

findings.title: Duplicate slice names produce first-wins versus last-wins metadata
findings.claim: Neither loader rejects duplicate slice names. Python's metadata dictionary keeps the last definition, while Rust searches definitions and keeps the first, yielding different accepted artifacts.
findings.reproduction: I configured two slices named `App`: the first matched `/app` and had `metadata.owner: first`; the second matched `/other` and had `metadata.owner: second`. For one `/app/x.py` sample, both CLIs exited 0 and attributed the time to `App`, but Python emitted `metadata: {"owner":"second"}` and Rust emitted `metadata: {"owner":"first"}`.

findings.title: Facts-v2 profile metadata is defaulted or coerced instead of validated
findings.claim: Required selection metadata can disappear and change the selected metric, while non-string value-type fields are silently accepted and replay differently across languages.
findings.reproduction: I used a v2 artifact with `value_types` `[samples/count,cpu/ns]`, one sample `values:[1,100]`, `summary:null`, and omitted `profile.primary_value_index`. Both CLIs accepted it and projected total 1, although the documented fallback selection is the last declared type and therefore 100; an exporter-written index of 1 produces 100. Separately, with `value_types:[{"type":true,"unit":7}]`, `period_type:{"type":false,"unit":8}`, and `default_sample_type:true`, Python accepted and replayed strings `"True"`, `"7"`, `"False"`, and `"8"`; Rust accepted the same input but replayed empty strings for all four fields.

findings.title: Compare trusts self-inconsistent percentages and fails open
findings.claim: The gate validates and compares only supplied percentage fields; it never checks them against each row's `time_ns` and `summary.total_time_ns`. A corrupt or manipulated report can therefore hide an arbitrary regression.
findings.reproduction: I compared two `clankerprof_slices` reports with total 100. The `App` row changed from `time_ns:10` to `time_ns:90`, but both supplied `pct:10.0`; I used `--threshold-abs 0 --threshold-rel 0`. Both CLIs exited 0 and emitted `{"has_regression":false}` with row `{"name":"App","before_pct":10.0,"after_pct":10.0,"delta_abs":0.0,"delta_rel":0.0,"status":"stable"}`. Thus a ninefold increase with an unchanged total bypassed the gate.

findings.title: Rust's core-class CSV parser still breaks multiline quoted fields
findings.claim: The specification promises Python `csv.reader` default semantics, but Rust splits the input into lines before parsing fields, so a valid quoted field containing a newline is split into two records.
findings.reproduction:
I wrote the valid CSV record `"Weird
Class"
`, then projected a native leaf named `Weird
Class#run` under target `Parent` with that file supplied through `--core-classes`. Python exited 0 with categories `[["Ruby Core (Native)",10]]`; Rust exited 0 with `[["Other",10]]`.

findings.title: Delimiter-based aggregation keys merge distinct frames and caller/leaf pairs
findings.claim: Several maps encode tuples by concatenating unrestricted profile strings with a delimiter. Legitimately distinct symbols containing that delimiter collide and have their evidence merged under the first identity.
findings.reproduction: For slices, I supplied 10 ns for `(function="A", filename="B\u0000C")` and 20 ns for `(function="A\u0000B", filename="C")`. Both CLIs emitted one frame identified as `A`/`B\u0000C` with `time_ns:30`, rather than two frames. For scope caller evidence, pairs `(caller="A -> B", leaf="C")` and `(caller="A", leaf="B -> C")` likewise collapsed to one `caller_leaf_pairs` row `{"pair":"A -> B -> C","samples":2,"time_ns":30}`.

findings.title: Both protobuf decoders accept illegal field-zero and invalid-UTF-8 data
findings.claim: The advertised strict decoder does not validate protobuf field numbers or UTF-8 string fields. Malformed profiles are converted into ordinary facts rather than rejected.
findings.reproduction: I passed raw bytes `\x00\x00` to `facts --profile`; field number zero is illegal protobuf, but both CLIs exited 0 and emitted an ordinary empty `clankerprof.sample_facts.v2` artifact. I then passed `\x32\x01\xff`, a string-table field containing invalid UTF-8; both exited 0 and emitted `profile.default_sample_type:"\ufffd"` instead of a decode error.

summary: Found 12 new reproduced issues: 9 major and 3 minor; none merely re-raises an R1-R4 finding. `./bin/dev check` passed, and `./bin/dev test-rust` passed while running the workspace Rust tests. A separate literal `cargo test --workspace` attempt was blocked before execution because the read-only audit sandbox denied `target/debug/.cargo-build-lock`; that is environmental and not listed as a repository finding. The green gates do not cover the cases above.
